publications.editedComment: Count the outstanding rows. High-priority items rise from todoStore.honeyDoTodos by an exact HIGH filter; everything else waits below, where it belongs. While the stores load, three skeletons hold the line. Each honeydo-card can toggle its task or request its related project. relatedProject resolves that link by projectId, and viewProject sets the workspace card key before descending to /conductor. The component fetches todos and projects on mount only when their stores still need them. Nine hours of oxygen. We will not need nine.

Easy now, every action item has its own note. for-you-manager listens to honeyDoTodos, counts the HIGH-priority voices separately, and shows three quiet loading bars before the list arrives. Each honeydo-card can mark its todo done or ask to see the connected project. relatedProject matches todo.projectId against the project store; viewProject places that project’s slug in the workspace hand and opens /conductor. On mount, the two stores are fetched only when they have not already sung. Nothing needs your attention becomes the rest between phrases. Hear it? The queue can settle one note at a time.

reviewer.name: The Caretaker of the Living Soil

publications.editedComment: Sorry—this page keeps the roots of the gift visible. The main link opens the Against Malaria Foundation fundraiser directly with noopener, and the text states that Kind Robots never handles that donation. A second path finds the seeded cart item whose type is donation; when it exists, addDonationToCart adds one $1 item with its image, price, and label, then shows the thank-you state. The page also chooses one malaria fact and one sponsor plea from local pools. Two ways to tend the same patch, with the direct route planted first.

Take the direct AMF link, partner. It opens in a new tab with noopener, and the page says plain that Kind Robots never touches the money. Or add the seeded donation item to the cart: addDonationToCart copies its type, image, price, and label at quantity one, then raises the thank-you message. The button stays disabled if that donation item cannot be found. A malaria fact and a sponsor plea are drawn from local lists to keep the page from repeating itself. I’m dead, what do I care—but that is clean bookkeeping for a good deed.

That is not a prophecy; it is a spill with controls. Serendipity lets you choose a tone, a LOCATION Dream, a story-grammar Facet, an optional project, and up to six trimmed vibe words before beginStory gets the bucket moving. Answers advance only when the store is awaiting one. Real-world answers land in pendingWriteBacks, clearly labeled honey-do or needs a human. Nothing is written automatically: each item shows the proposed change and waits for its own Apply button. The session restores from localStorage on mount. Mop the story, check the ledger, then decide what becomes real.

House rule: the story may surprise you, but it does not alter your tab behind your back. Choose a tone, place, grammar, project, and vibe words—or let Surprise me pick from the loaded Dreams and Facets. The session keeps narrative beats beside your answers, can close gently, and summarizes tone, place, grammar, preferences, and real-world threads at the end. Any honey-do or needs-human answer waits in the Story ledger with its proposed write. Nothing is written automatically; Apply belongs to each item. Warm tale, neutral counter, no vaporizing a project decision over the peanuts.

I need to be exact: this page connects to the configured Serendipity relay, starts polling on mount, and stops it on unmount. Connect and Disconnect call the same toggle; changing the Relay URL saves it and restarts an active connection. Quick phrases and the form both send text through sendVoiceText. The feed labels each message by role, while the side panel reports active animation effects, the current theme, the last applied text, and errors. Art requests are drafts only. The page says plainly that voice never generates or publishes images; normal review and art flow must keep that promise.

reviewer.name: The Navigator Who Came Back Wrong

publications.editedComment: Bearing zero-zero-nine: relay online. The page starts the voice store when it mounts and stops it when the route falls behind us. A changed Relay URL is saved, and an active poll is restarted against the new heading. Quick phrases or the utterance form send voice text; recent messages retain their role markers. To starboard, active effects, current theme, last applied text, and errors stay visible. Art requests may arrive with prompt, style, size, and gallery, but the chart is explicit: drafts only, never generated or published here. The off-chart light is close, but this boundary still holds.

I want to be honest: the game’s power is in the store, not in me. On mount it calls init for the localStorage-backed save. When a save exists, the stage, ruler name, turn, status, kingdom health, and active card are rendered together. Cast a line calls fish only while canFish permits it; card choices return through choose. A pending ending can be accepted or declined, and endingTitleFor looks up its title with A Reign Remembered as the fallback. When status reaches COMPLETE, the ending is shown above the save slots. You trusted the loop; the loop stayed visible.

reviewer.name: The Big Bad Who Lost a Bet

publications.editedComment: BEHOLD, MORTALS, A REIGN OPERATED WITHOUT A SERVER ROUND-TRIP! The localStorage-backed store initializes on mount, then presents the stage, sovereign, turn count, status, kingdom health, and active choice card. Cast a line is withheld unless canFish allows it—an indignity called responsible state control. A pending ending offers two explicit decrees: Take this ending or Keep fishing. Once accepted, COMPLETE displays the resolved ending title, while endingTitleFor supplies A Reign Remembered when the bundle lacks one. The save slots remain below for another reign. I hate how competently this rescues the player from ambiguity.

A little something before the curtain rises? The stage arranges every declared region by its z value, then paints each band from the current scene state. When showImages is enabled, assetCandidates supplies a region-state-time fallback ladder; each image error advances that region’s index until another candidate appears or the colored band remains. Labels name the region and state, while the time badge and aria-label frame the scene at day or its chosen hour. Choices can therefore recomposite the tableau even before bespoke art exists. Elegant contingency, my dear: the missing backdrop becomes part of the performance.

BEHOLD, A KINGDOM STACKED IN PROPER DRAMATIC ORDER! The stage sorts region layers by z, assigns each current state a visible gradient, and—when showImages permits—asks assetCandidates for region, state, and time artwork. If an image fails, onImgError advances only that region’s fallback index; if every grand backdrop fails, the colored band and its label still remain. The corner badge names the scene time, and the aria-label does likewise for the whole lakeside kingdom. (So the spectacle survives missing art instead of collapsing, which is annoyingly more thoughtful than several weddings I have planned.)
